title: Unit-Economics Decomposition

content.statement: Overall economics are too blunt; you need to know exactly which sub-components create or destroy value.
content.question: Can you name every layer of cost between a business signup and a profit dollar? What's the biggest surprise?
content.equation: UE = Lifetime Revenue − CAC − Variable Service Cost − Retention Cost − Support Burden
sop: Calculate lifetime value, total revenue or profit expected from an average customer over their relationship with you (see calculation, page 6).
Calculate acquisition cost, your average cost to acquire one paying customer (see calculation, page 6).
Add up Variable Service Cost, Retention Cost, and Support Burden per customer, every dollar spent serving, keeping, and supporting them (see calculation, page 6).
Subtract all four cost layers from lifetime value to get true unit economics per customer (see calculation, page 6).
Rebuild your pricing or service model around whichever layer is the biggest drag once you see the real number.
content.explainer: Good aggregate numbers can hide bad underlying economics. Decompose or die.
questions: Can you name every layer of cost between a business signup and a profit dollar? What's the biggest surprise?
If you added up every dollar it costs to serve a customer after the sale, would your "profitable" customers still be profitable?
Which single cost layer, acquisition, service, retention, or support, is quietly eating the most margin, and have you ever measured it directly?

definitions.def: Lifetime Value, the total revenue or profit you expect from a customer over the entire relationship.
Customer Acquisition Cost, what you spend on average to gain one new paying customer.
The per-customer or per-transaction profit and cost breakdown. Healthy unit economics = each customer is profitable on their own.

content.equation_example: Using gross lifetime revenue per customer $400, minus CAC $80, Variable Service $50, Retention $20, Support $15. UE = 400 − 80 − 50 − 20 − 15 = $235 profit per customer. Positive and growing means each customer truly pays off; near zero or negative means you're buying customers who never become profitable.
content.closing_statement: Every profit dollar has to survive several deductions first.

caption:
Principle Nº 51: Unit-Economics Decomposition

Overall economics are too blunt; you need to know exactly which sub-components create or destroy value.

Ask yourself: Can you name every layer of cost between a business signup and a profit dollar? What's the biggest surprise?
